title: Planning the Internal Audit Activity
description: Understand how to develop risk-based audit plans and manage resources

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: The annual audit plan determines WHERE internal audit focuses resources. A risk-based plan ensures limited audit resources address the most significant risks. This is fundamental CAE responsibility!

sections.title: Risk-Based Audit Planning
sections.content:
**Standard 2010:**

The CAE must establish a risk-based plan to determine the priorities of the internal audit activity, consistent with the organization's goals.

**Key elements:**
• Aligned with organizational strategy
• Based on risk assessment
• Considers input from senior management and Board
• Reviewed and adjusted as needed

sections.title: Risk Assessment for Planning
sections.content:
**Standard 2010.A1:**

The internal audit activity's plan must be based on a documented risk assessment, undertaken at least annually.

**Risk factors to consider:**
• Impact (financial, reputational, operational)
• Likelihood of occurrence
• Velocity (speed of impact)
• Time since last audit
• Management concerns
• Regulatory requirements
• Strategic importance

sections.title: The Audit Universe
sections.content:
**Definition:**
All possible auditable areas within the organization.

**May include:**
• Business units and locations
• Processes and functions
• Systems and applications
• Projects and initiatives
• Compliance areas
• Third-party relationships

**Best practice:**
• Update at least annually
• Consider organizational changes
• Align with business structure

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Risk-Based Planning
sections.content:
**"DIAL"** for prioritization:

**D**irect risk factors (impact, likelihood)
**I**nterval since last audit
**A**udit resources available
**L**egislative/regulatory requirements

*Dial up the audits that matter most!*

sections.title: Communicating the Plan
sections.content:
**Standard 2020:**

The CAE must communicate the internal audit activity's plans and resource requirements to senior management and the Board for review and approval.

**Also communicate:**
• Significant interim changes
• Impact of resource limitations
• Any scope limitations

**Board approval required:**
• Annual audit plan
• Significant plan changes
• Resource requirements

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Audit plan must be risk-based and documented
Risk assessment required at least annually
Board approves the annual audit plan
Audit universe = all auditable entities
Consider: impact, likelihood, time since last audit, resources
Communicate resource limitations and changes to Board

title: Resource Management
description: Understand resource allocation, staffing, and outsourcing considerations

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Internal audit must have the RIGHT people with the RIGHT skills to execute the audit plan. Understanding resource options (in-house, co-source, outsource) is critical for CAE success.

sections.title: Resource Management Requirements
sections.content:
**Standard 2030:**

The CAE must ensure that internal audit resources are appropriate, sufficient, and effectively deployed to achieve the approved plan.

**Resources include:**
• Personnel (staff)
• Technology and tools
• Financial budget
• Time allocation

sections.title: Sourcing Options
sections.content:
**In-House:**
• Full-time IA staff
• Direct control and oversight
• Organizational knowledge

**Co-Sourcing:**
• Supplement internal staff with external
• Obtain specialized skills
• Flexible capacity
• CAE retains responsibility

**Outsourcing:**
• External provider performs IA
• Organization retains oversight through CAE or designated
• Less direct control
• May be cost-effective for small organizations

sections.title: When to Co-Source
sections.content:
**Common reasons:**

• **Specialized skills** - IT, fraud, forensic, actuarial
• **Capacity shortfall** - Too much work, not enough staff
• **Independence** - Need external perspective
• **Geographic coverage** - Remote locations
• **One-time projects** - Special investigations

**CAE responsibilities:**
• Define scope of work
• Supervise the work
• Review deliverables
• Ensure quality standards

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: External Service Provider
sections.content:
**When using external providers:**

✅ Organization RETAINS responsibility for IA
✅ CAE or designee oversees the work
✅ External assessments still required (every 5 years)
✅ Must confirm provider competence and independence

❌ Cannot "outsource" responsibility for IA
❌ Provider cannot assess their own work

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: CAE ensures resources are appropriate, sufficient, effectively deployed
Consider: competency, coverage, capacity, continuity
Sourcing options: In-house, Co-source, Outsource
Co-sourcing provides specialized skills and flexible capacity
Organization retains responsibility even when outsourcing

title: Policies, Procedures, and Coordination
description: Understand internal audit policies, procedures, and coordination with other assurance providers

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Consistent policies and procedures ensure quality and efficiency. Coordinating with other assurance providers (external audit, risk, compliance) maximizes coverage and minimizes duplication!

sections.title: Policies and Procedures
sections.content:
**Standard 2040:**

The CAE must establish policies and procedures to guide the internal audit activity.

**Purpose:**
• Ensure consistency
• Provide direction to staff
• Document methodology
• Support quality

**Form and content:**
• Should be appropriate for size and structure of IA
• May be formal manual or automated tools

sections.title: Coordination with Other Providers
sections.content:
**Standard 2050:**

The CAE should share information, coordinate activities, and consider relying upon the work of other internal and external assurance and consulting service providers.

**Benefits of coordination:**
• Reduce duplication of effort
• Maximize coverage
• Share information and insights
• Improve efficiency

sections.title: Coordination with External Audit
sections.content:
**Key coordination activities:**

• Share audit plans
• Discuss risk assessments
• Share workpapers (where appropriate)
• Avoid duplicate testing
• Coordinate timing

**Reliance considerations:**
• External auditor may rely on IA work
• IA may rely on external auditor work
• Must evaluate competence and objectivity
• Must evaluate work performed

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Coordination Partners
sections.content:
**"SCREAM"** - Who does IA coordinate with?

**S**OX compliance team
**C**ompliance department
**R**isk management
**E**xternal auditors
**A**ssurance providers (other)
**M**anagement (line functions)

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: CAE establishes policies and procedures for IA consistency
Policies should fit size and structure of organization
Coordinate with other assurance providers to reduce duplication
May rely on work of others after evaluating competence
Share information with external auditors where appropriate

title: Engagement Planning Fundamentals
description: Understand the planning process for individual audit engagements

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Engagement planning sets the stage for a successful audit. Poor planning leads to scope creep, missed risks, and wasted effort. Master the planning standards - they're heavily tested!

sections.title: Engagement Planning Requirements
sections.content:
**Standard 2200:**

Internal auditors must develop and document a plan for each engagement.

**Planning includes:**
• Objectives
• Scope
• Timing
• Resource allocation

**Pre-engagement research:**
• Review prior audits
• Understand business context
• Identify key risks and controls

sections.title: Engagement Objectives
sections.content:
**Standard 2210:**

Objectives must be established for each engagement.

**Standard 2210.A1:**

Internal auditors must conduct a preliminary assessment of risks relevant to the activity under review. Engagement objectives must reflect results of this assessment.

**Objective types:**
• Compliance (with laws, policies)
• Operations (efficiency, effectiveness)
• Financial (accuracy, reliability)
• Strategic (alignment with goals)

sections.title: Engagement Scope
sections.content:
**Standard 2220:**

The established scope must be sufficient to achieve the objectives.

**Scope includes:**
• Systems and processes covered
• Time period reviewed
• Locations included
• Types of testing

**Standard 2220.A1:**
Scope must include consideration of relevant systems, records, personnel, and physical properties.

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Planning Framework - "OSTRAS"
sections.content:
**O**bjectives - What are we trying to achieve?
**S**cope - What's included/excluded?
**T**iming - When will work be performed?
**R**esources - Who will do the work?
**A**pproach - How will we test?
**S**upervision - Who will oversee?

*"OSTRAS" - the framework for planning success!*

sections.title: Resource Allocation
sections.content:
**Standard 2230:**

Internal auditors must determine appropriate and sufficient resources to achieve engagement objectives based on an evaluation of the nature and complexity of each engagement, time constraints, and available resources.

**Consider:**
• Auditor competency for this area
• Number of staff needed
• Estimated hours
• Technology and tools required
• External expertise needed

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Planning must be documented for each engagement
Preliminary survey informs objectives and scope
Objectives reflect preliminary risk assessment
Scope must be sufficient to achieve objectives
Resource allocation considers complexity and constraints

title: Engagement Work Programs
description: Understand how to develop and document work programs

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Work programs are the ROADMAP for fieldwork. They translate objectives into specific procedures. A well-designed program ensures consistent, complete testing.

sections.title: Work Program Requirements
sections.content:
**Standard 2240:**

Internal auditors must develop and document work programs that achieve the engagement objectives.

**Work program elements:**
• Procedures for identifying, analyzing, evaluating, and documenting information
• Approved prior to implementation
• Any adjustments approved promptly

**Purpose:**
• Guide fieldwork
• Ensure completeness
• Provide basis for supervision

sections.title: Designing Procedures
sections.content:
**Each procedure should:**

• Relate to an engagement objective
• Specify the nature of testing
• Define the sample or population
• Indicate expected evidence
• Allow for documentation of results

**Procedure types:**
• Inquiry and observation
• Inspection and examination
• Re-performance and recalculation
• Analytical procedures
• Confirmation

sections.title: Work Program Structure
rows: What the engagement aims to achieve

sections.title: Procedure Design Example
sections.content:
**Objective:** Verify vendor payments are properly authorized

**Procedure:**
1. Select sample of 25 payments over $10,000
2. Obtain supporting documentation
3. Verify approved purchase order exists
4. Confirm invoice matches PO terms
5. Verify appropriate management approval
6. Document results and exceptions

**Expected evidence:** Approved PO, invoice, approval signature

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Procedure Types - "CIRCA"
sections.content:
**C**onfirmation - Third-party verification
**I**nquiry - Ask questions
**R**e-performance - Redo the process
**C**omputation - Recalculate
**A**nalysis - Compare, trend, ratio

*Plus: Inspection, Observation*

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Work programs must be documented and approved
Procedures should link to objectives
Adjustments require prompt approval
Programs guide fieldwork and enable supervision
Procedure types: Inquiry, Inspection, Re-performance, Analysis, Confirmation

title: Gathering and Evaluating Information
description: Understand information gathering techniques and evidence evaluation

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Evidence is the FOUNDATION of audit conclusions. Weak evidence = weak conclusions. Understanding evidence types, sufficiency, and reliability is core to professional auditing!

sections.title: Information Requirements
sections.content:
**Standard 2310:**

Internal auditors must identify sufficient, reliable, relevant, and useful information to achieve the engagement's objectives.

**SRRU criteria:**
• **Sufficient** - Enough to support conclusions
• **Reliable** - Trustworthy, verifiable
• **Relevant** - Relates to objectives
• **Useful** - Helps achieve objectives

sections.title: Evidence Reliability Factors
sections.content:
**More reliable when:**

• **External source** > Internal source
• **Written** > Oral
• **Original** > Copy
• **Direct observation** > Indirect report
• **Corroborated** > Uncorroborated
• **Strong internal control** > Weak control

**Less reliable:**
• Hearsay
• Self-reported without verification
• From parties with vested interest

sections.title: Information Gathering Techniques
sections.content:
**Inquiry:**
• Ask questions of knowledgeable parties
• Obtain understanding of processes
• Must corroborate with other evidence

**Observation:**
• Watch processes being performed
• Point-in-time evidence only
• Document what and when observed

**Inspection:**
• Examine records and documents
• Verify existence and attributes
• Select samples appropriately

**Confirmation:**
• Obtain direct response from third parties
• Highly reliable for verifying external claims

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Evidence Quality - "SRRU"
sections.content:
**S**ufficient - Enough quantity
**R**eliable - Can be trusted
**R**elevant - Relates to objective
**U**seful - Helps achieve purpose

*"SRRU" = "Sure, Really, Really, Useful!"*

sections.title: Evaluating Sufficiency
sections.content:
**Factors affecting sufficiency:**

• Materiality of the area
• Risk level (higher risk = more evidence)
• Quality of available evidence
• Professional judgment
• Cost-benefit consideration

**Insufficient evidence:**
• Note scope limitation
• Seek alternative procedures
• Consider impact on conclusions

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Evidence must be SRRU: Sufficient, Reliable, Relevant, Useful
Physical and documentary evidence more reliable than testimonial
External sources more reliable than internal
Corroborate testimonial evidence with other sources
Higher risk areas require more evidence

title: Analysis and Evaluation
description: Understand analytical procedures and evaluation of control effectiveness

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Analysis transforms raw data into meaningful conclusions. Knowing how to apply analytical procedures and interpret results is what separates data collection from professional auditing!

sections.title: Analysis Requirements
sections.content:
**Standard 2320:**

Internal auditors must base conclusions and engagement results on appropriate analyses and evaluations.

**Analysis process:**
1. Gather evidence
2. Organize and classify
3. Analyze patterns and relationships
4. Compare to criteria
5. Draw conclusions
6. Develop recommendations

sections.title: Control Testing Approaches
sections.content:
**Testing control design:**
• "Will the control prevent/detect the risk?"
• Review documentation, walkthroughs
• Evaluate design effectiveness

**Testing control operation:**
• "Is the control actually working?"
• Examine evidence of performance
• Sample-based testing
• Re-performance

sections.title: Evaluating Findings
sections.content:
**For each finding, assess:**

**Condition** - What exists (the facts)
**Criteria** - What should exist (the standard)
**Cause** - Why the difference exists
**Effect** - Impact of the condition

**Significance factors:**
• Frequency of occurrence
• Financial impact
• Risk to organization
• Systemic vs isolated

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Finding Elements - "4 Cs"
sections.content:
**C**ondition - What IS happening
**C**riteria - What SHOULD happen
**C**ause - WHY it happened
**C**onsequence (Effect) - What's the IMPACT

*Every finding needs all 4 Cs!*

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Conclusions must be based on appropriate analysis
Analytical procedures: Trend, ratio, reasonableness, variance, benchmark
Test both control design and operating effectiveness
Findings have 4 elements: Condition, Criteria, Cause, Effect
Assess significance of findings for reporting

title: Documenting Engagement Results
description: Understand workpaper documentation standards and practices

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Documentation is the PROOF of your work. Workpapers support conclusions, enable supervision, and provide organizational memory. Poor documentation = indefensible audit!

sections.title: Documentation Requirements
sections.content:
**Standard 2330:**

Internal auditors must document sufficient, reliable, relevant, and useful information to support the engagement results and conclusions.

**Purpose of workpapers:**
• Support observations and recommendations
• Provide evidence of work performed
• Enable supervision and review
• Facilitate future audits
• Defend against challenges

sections.title: Workpaper Content
sections.content:
**Essential elements:**

• **Heading:** Engagement name, preparer, date
• **Objective:** What the work addresses
• **Procedures:** What was done
• **Results:** What was found
• **Conclusions:** What it means
• **References:** Cross-references to other workpapers
• **Review:** Evidence of supervisory review

sections.title: Documentation Quality Attributes
rows: Connected to source and other papers

sections.title: Record Retention and Control
sections.content:
**Standard 2330.A1:**

The CAE must control access to engagement records and obtain approval before releasing externally.

**Standard 2330.A2:**

The CAE must develop retention requirements for engagement records, consistent with organization guidelines and regulations.

**Best practices:**
• Secure storage (physical and electronic)
• Access controls
• Retention schedules
• Disposition procedures

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: External Release
sections.content:
**Before releasing workpapers externally:**

✅ Obtain CAE and/or senior management approval
✅ Consider legal/regulatory requirements
✅ Consult legal counsel as appropriate
✅ Document the approval and release

**Common external requestors:**
• External auditors
• Regulators
• Legal proceedings

*Always control release and document approval!*

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Documentation must support engagement conclusions
Workpapers should be complete, accurate, clear, concise
CAE controls access and approves external release
Retention requirements must comply with regulations
Documentation enables supervision and future reference

title: Engagement Supervision
description: Understand supervision requirements and practices throughout the engagement

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Supervision ensures quality throughout the engagement, not just at the end. It's the front-line quality control that prevents errors and develops staff!

sections.title: Supervision Requirements
sections.content:
**Standard 2340:**

Engagements must be properly supervised to ensure objectives are achieved, quality is assured, and staff is developed.

**Supervision occurs throughout:**
• Planning
• Fieldwork
• Reporting
• Follow-up

sections.title: Supervision Activities
rows: Monitor progress, review workpapers, guide staff

sections.title: Factors Affecting Supervision Level
sections.content:
**More supervision needed when:**

• Staff is less experienced
• Engagement is complex
• Area is high risk
• New audit area
• Sensitive subject matter

**Less supervision needed when:**
• Experienced staff
• Routine audit
• Lower risk area
• Established audit program

sections.title: Documentation of Supervision
sections.content:
**Evidence of supervision:**

• Reviewer initials and dates on workpapers
• Review notes addressed and cleared
• Sign-off on key conclusions
• File review checklists
• Coaching and development discussions documented

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Supervision required throughout engagement lifecycle
Level of supervision varies by risk and staff experience
Supervision ensures quality and develops staff
Document evidence of supervisory review
Address review notes before finalizing

title: Communicating Engagement Results
description: Understand communication requirements, report elements, and quality criteria

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: The audit report is the PRIMARY deliverable. All the work leads to this communication. A poorly written report undermines the entire engagement. Master the communication standards!

sections.title: Communication Requirements
sections.content:
**Standard 2400:**

Internal auditors must communicate the results of engagements.

**Standard 2410:**

Communications must include:
• Engagement objectives
• Scope
• Applicable conclusions
• Recommendations and action plans

sections.title: Communication Quality Criteria
sections.content:
**Standard 2420:**

Communications must be accurate, objective, clear, concise, constructive, complete, and timely.

**AOCCCCT:**
• **Accurate** - Free from errors
• **Objective** - Fair, unbiased
• **Clear** - Easily understood
• **Concise** - To the point
• **Constructive** - Helpful, improvement-focused
• **Complete** - All significant information
• **Timely** - Appropriately timed

sections.title: Report Elements
rows: What the engagement aimed to achieve
What was covered and time period

sections.title: Opinions and Ratings
sections.content:
**Standard 2410.A1:**

Final communication of engagement results must include, where appropriate:
• Auditor's opinion and/or conclusion
• Recommendations for potential improvements

**Opinion types:**
• Overall assessment of control effectiveness
• Ratings (satisfactory, needs improvement, unsatisfactory)
• Opinion on compliance with requirements

**Basis for opinion:**
• Must be supported by sufficient evidence
• Explain basis and limitations

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Communication Quality - "AOCCCCT"
sections.content:
**A**ccurate - No errors
**O**bjective - Not biased
**C**lear - Easy to understand
**C**oncise - Not wordy
**C**onstructive - Helpful
**C**omplete - Nothing missing
**T**imely - Right time

*"A Outstanding Communicator Creates Crisp Complete Timeliness"*

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Reports must include objectives, scope, conclusions, recommendations
Quality criteria: Accurate, Objective, Clear, Concise, Constructive, Complete, Timely
Opinions must be supported by sufficient evidence
Include management response and action plans
Disseminate to appropriate parties

title: Monitoring Progress and Follow-Up
description: Understand requirements for monitoring implementation of corrective actions

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: An audit isn't complete until findings are addressed! Follow-up closes the loop and ensures value is delivered. Without it, recommendations become a to-do list that never gets done.

sections.title: Follow-Up Requirements
sections.content:
**Standard 2500:**

The CAE must establish and maintain a system to monitor the disposition of results communicated to management.

**Standard 2500.A1:**

The CAE must establish a follow-up process to monitor and ensure that management actions have been effectively implemented or that senior management has accepted the risk of not taking action.

sections.title: Tracking and Escalation
sections.content:
**Tracking system should include:**
• Finding/recommendation description
• Risk rating
• Owner/responsible party
• Due date
• Current status
• Verification notes

**Escalation triggers:**
• Past due items
• High-risk items unaddressed
• Repeated deferrals
• Risk acceptance by senior management

sections.title: Risk Acceptance
sections.content:
**Standard 2600:**

When the CAE concludes that management has accepted a level of risk that may be unacceptable to the organization, the CAE must discuss the matter with senior management. If the CAE determines that the matter has not been resolved, the CAE must communicate the matter to the Board.

**Board communication required when:**
• Significant risk remains unaddressed
• Management accepts risk exceeding tolerance
• Unresolved disagreement with management

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Unresolved Risks
sections.content:
**Key escalation points:**

1. First: Discuss with Senior Management
2. If unresolved: Communicate to Board

**CAE cannot force management to act, but MUST escalate to Board if risk is unacceptable!**

*Board is the final escalation point for unaddressed significant risks.*

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: CAE must establish follow-up system
Verify implementation of corrective actions
Track status of all open recommendations
Escalate unresolved significant risks to Board
Report follow-up status to Board regularly
